practice: Estimate the time complexity of three short snippets you write.
Explain why binary search is O(log n) in your own words.
Compare O(n log n) and O(n^2) for n=1,000 and n=1,000,000.
title: Algorithms Lesson 1: Big-O Fundamentals
references: Introduction to Algorithms (Cormen et al.), Chapter 2
Big-O Cheat Sheet (overview of common complexities)
summary: Learn how to describe algorithms, measure complexity, and compare growth rates.
content:
# Algorithms Lesson 1: Big-O Fundamentals

## Overview
Algorithms are repeatable procedures for solving problems. Big-O notation gives a high-level upper bound on how runtime grows as input size n grows.

## Big-O in plain language
- Focus on growth rate, not exact timing
- Drop constants and lower-order terms
- Use worst-case unless stated otherwise

## Common growth rates
- O(1) constant time
- O(log n) logarithmic (binary search)
- O(n) linear (simple scan)
- O(n log n) efficient sorting
- O(n^2) quadratic (nested loops)

## Estimating complexity
1. Identify loops and how many times they run
2. Multiply for nested loops, add for sequential blocks
3. Keep the dominant term and drop constants

## Example: search choices
Use linear search for unsorted data. Use binary search only when the data is sorted and random access is fast.
examples: Analyze a double loop and explain why it is O(n^2).
Compare linear search vs binary search for n=1,024.
Decide whether preprocessing a list for faster queries is worth it.
objectives: Define algorithmic complexity and input size.
Estimate the complexity of simple code blocks.
Choose between algorithms using growth rates.
